title: Deformation Behavior of Polystyrene-Grafted Nanoparticle Assemblies with Low Grafting Density
abstract: Matrix-free, polymer-grafted nanoparticle (PGN) assemblies show promise for a wide array of structural, photonic, and electrical applications. We examine the modulus, yield strength, and crazing of assemblies of polystyrene grafted  $Fe_3O_4$  ( $Fe_3O_4$ -PS) at low graft density ( $A3$ < 0.15 chains/nm<sup>2</sup>) where chain entanglements are maximized. From the wrinkling-cracking method (WCM) we show that modulus (E) and yield stress  $(C3_v)$  are independent of nanoscale film thickness  $(70 < h_f < 250 	ext{ nm})$ and graft molecular weight (30 kDa < MW < 370 kDa) and in good agreement with predictions from effective medium theory. Furthermore, thin film craze observations from TEM imply two critical length scales for maximum deformability of matrix-free PGN assemblies: (1) PGN core size should be less than the critical length scale of the craze microstructure, and (2) near-neighbor entanglements are optimized for the lowest graft length (N) where the PGN architecture exhibit intermediate graft densities ( $r_0A3^{0.5}C1 3-6$  and  $N/N_eC1 4-6$ ). These findings provide a foundation for optimizing designs that simultaneously maximize inorganic volume fraction, processability, and mechanical robustness for PGN thin film assemblies.
results: PGNs with canopy dominated by SDPB conformations ( $r_c C1 r_0$ ) were synthesized via surface-initiated reversible addition—fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization of polystyrene (PS) from iron oxide nanoparticles ( $r_0 = 3.85 B1 0.55$  nm). In brief, iron oxide nanoparticles were first synthesized and stabilized with oleic acid and oleylamine. A carboxyl-terminated trithiocarbonate was used as a chain transfer agent (CTA) and incorporated onto a triethoxysilane and anchored onto the NPs via ligand exchange. Finally, RAFT polymerization was performed from the surface with styrene to synthesize low graft density Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>-PS PGNs. Graft density and molecular weight could be readily tailored by varying polymerization time and RAFT agent concentrations. Table 1 summarizes the characteristics of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>-PS PGNs discussed ( $A3 < 0.15$  nm<sup>-2</sup>, 30 kDa < MW < 370 kDa).
conclusion: Ascertaining the constraints on PGN architecture imposed by satisfactory mechanical performance is crucial to developing these materials for numerous applications. To complement prior reports of matrix free PGN assemblies with large core and graft density, we examined PGNs assemblies of small core and low graft density (Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>-PS PGNs:  $r_0 = 3.85$  nm,  $A3 < 0.15$ chains/nm<sup>2</sup>). The brush scaling of these sparsely grafted PGNs agreed favorably with solution PGN models in good solvents (toluene) confirming SDPB canopies  $(r_c C1 r_0)$ . As anticipated, such relaxed solution conformations enabled extensive entanglement between neighoring PGNs and continuous assemblies. Modulus (E) and yield stress ( $C3_{v}$ ) were independent of film thickness (70 <  $h_{m f}$  < 250 nm), confirming observations were not perturbed by surface confinement.  $E_{m PGN}/E_{m PS} C1 1.11$  and  $C3_{m y,PGN}/C3_{m y,PS} C1 0.44$ , which are in good agreement with effective medium theory (Hashin-Shtrikman (HS) lower bound) and local stress enhancement due to the different moduli of the nanoparticle and PS. Similar to pure PS, a transition from brittle fracture to crazing occurs for grafts >110 kDa, and stable craze extension is seen above 200 kDa. From these experiments, two critical length scales are deduced that bound deformability of matrix free PGN assemblies. First, undeformable PGN core should be less than the critical length scale of the craze microstructure to avoid nanoparticle jamming at the craze boundary. Second, the minimum chain length to form near-neighbor entanglements occurs at intermediate graft densities ( $r_0 A3^{0.5} C1 3-6$  and  $N/N_e C1 4-6$ ).
